text.title: Fixture Design Patterns
text.summary: Factory, Builder, and Object Mother patterns for test data creation. When to use each and their anti-patterns.

list.item: Factory — creates objects with sensible defaults, overridable per call. Best for: most cases.
Builder — fluent API for complex objects with many optional fields. Best for: orders, requests with many options.
Object Mother — named factory methods for semantically meaningful objects. Best for: domain-specific test scenarios.

section.title: Factory Pattern
section.rule: Factories set all required fields to valid defaults so tests only specify what matters.

example.code:
import factory from myapp.models import User, Product
class UserFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = User
email = factory.Sequence(lambda n: f"user{n}@example.com") name = factory.Faker("name") role = "user"
class ProductFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = Product
name = factory.Faker("catch_phrase") price = factory.Faker("pydecimal", left_digits=3, right_digits=2, positive=True) owner = factory.SubFactory(UserFactory)
# Usage: user = UserFactory(role="admin") # override single field product = ProductFactory.create_batch(5) # create 5 products

section.title: Builder Pattern
section.rule: Use the Builder when an object has many optional fields and multiple valid configurations.

section.title: Object Mother
section.rule: Object Mother provides semantically named factory methods that express domain intent.

antipattern.description: Mystery Guest: test data created invisibly in conftest or setUp, not visible in the test
antipattern.reason: Use factory fixtures with explicit parameters in each test instead.

antipattern.description: God Fixture: one fixture creates everything the test suite needs
antipattern.reason: Split into composable factory fixtures; tests request only what they need.
